data.title: 4 Questions For The AI 'True Believers'
data.description:
The AI narrative that has driven more equity gains for three years now continues to hold up despite more and more analysts and pundits expressing concerns around a potential AI bubble. My thesis continues to be that every major technology paradigm shift in U.S. history has resulted in a boom-and-bust cycle. This includes the birth of the railroads, electrification, automobiles, and the internet. I see no reason that the AI revolution will not follow a similar path. With the massive gains of the past few years, the AI juggernauts that have driven this rally, like Microsoft (MSFT) and NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA), seem to be in bubble territory. The only real question is whether we are in the fifth or sixth inning of this cycle or the eighth or ninth inning before the inevitable bust.

"True believers" that are betting this cycle is "different" and will not lead to a bust like every other paradigm shift should have confident answers to the following four questions around the sustainability of this AI-driven rally.

Question No. 1: Can OpenAI Keep Its Commitments?

Earlier this month, I penned an article on how dependent the entire AI ecosystem is around OpenAI. This non-public company should deliver $14 billion in revenues in 2025 but has made more than $1.4 trillion in commitments for future computing power. The company's CEO just called a "Code Red" to better focus the company on improving ChatGPT in light of slowing subscriptions, intensifying competition, as well as surging investment costs.

ChatGPT faces increasing competition from Alphabet (GOOG) on the consumer front, as that tech titan recently released Gemini 3, which has been well-received. Anthropic continues to dominate the corporate space. A new article on Zerohedge (unfortunately currently paywalled) took company projections and reckoned that OpenAI will take $140 billion in cumulative losses before it turns profitable in FY2029 or FY2030. To put it in perspective, this is 3.5 times the cumulative losses Tesla (TSLA) racked up from 2009 to 2022 before it became profitable. And Tesla's cumulative losses were multiple times higher than either Amazon or Alphabet booked before those internet giants achieved profitability. Given this, will OpenAI live up to its huge commitments in the years ahead?

Question No. 2: Will The Power Grid Support Massive New Data Centers?

One of the primary restraints around the AI revolution will come from the electrical grid. Massive data centers require a huge amount of 100% reliable electricity to operate. This nukes (pun intended) renewable energy sources like wind and solar to power these data centers.

The environmental lobby will continue to prevent new coal plants from being built for electricity generation, and to a lesser extent this applies to electrical generation from crude oil. This leaves largely natural gas generation. Fortunately, the United States has huge natural gas reserves. However, new capacity will take years to build out. Nuclear is another option, but historically this type of capacity takes at least a decade to bring online.

Jensen Huang, the CEO of NVIDIA, is among many talking up the potential of smaller modular nuclear reactors. This week Mr. Huang was talking up the potential of this new power source on the Joe Rogan show. However, even optimistically, he didn't see any of these reactors coming online until 2031 at the earliest.

Morgan Stanley has projected the electrical generation shortfall could reach up to the output of 44 average-sized nuclear reactors by 2028. The tailwinds for potential new nuclear capacity to meet data center demand have powered huge rises in related names like Oklo Inc. (OKLO) over the past year, it should be noted.

However, for the near term, new demand will need to be met with new natural gas generation capacity. Residential retail electricity prices in September were up 7.4% on a year-over-year basis. This is much higher than overall inflation, and prices are rising faster in states with higher data center concentration, such as Virginia, Maryland, and New Jersey. This is one driver of the increasing resistance in many locales to new data center projects. At least 16 data center projects, worth a combined $64 billion, have been blocked or delayed by this increasing grassroots resistance. This could be an increasing roadblock to new AI infrastructure in the quarters ahead.

Question No. 3: Will AI Revenue Growth Justify Huge AI Investments?

Just over $6.5 trillion will be spent in total on the buildout of AI infrastructure globally by 2030, according to a recent report from McKinsey & Co. Just over $3 trillion will go towards GPUs and other components going into these massive data centers.

The challenge is whether AI-related revenues will grow enough to even begin to justify these huge investments. AI-related revenues are expected to grow impressively but still come in at under $300 billion in FY2029. Will consumers pony up to pay for subscriptions for access to AI capabilities en masse. Microsoft 365 is the most successful subscription service in history. It has nearly 350 million paying subscribers that will generate almost $78 billion in revenues in FY2025. Will any AI service be nearly as successful?

Question No. 4: Is Depreciation Significantly Underestimating Costs?

Finally, we go to a recent new concern around the AI revolution and one I covered in granular detail in this recent article at the end of November. As I noted in that piece, the tech giants are largely moving to depreciating their AI infrastructure assets over a five-to-six-year life cycle. That may be fine for some data center components like routers and network equipment. However, for GPUs that will make up the majority of that spending, that seems more than aggressive and goes against historical depreciation schedules.

Advanced chipsets tend to have 2–3-year useful life cycles, and that has been the historical way to depreciate these assets, which mimics Moore's Law. NVIDIA has historically launched major upgrades to its core GPU line every two years. Intel (INTC) followed a similar schedule when it ruled the roost during the Internet Boom and PC Revolution.

Now these GPUs, if they don't burn out in 2-3 years of heavy use, could be repositioned to less critical tasks, maintaining some value. However, do investors expect most data centers to not upgrade to the latest and greatest GPUs when they become available to maintain their competitive position?

This is important, as by 2030, the AI tech giants could have a cumulative $2.5 trillion of capital investment in AI infrastructure subject to depreciation. A large chunk of this is around GPUs. On a five-year depreciation schedule, that is $500 billion of depreciation annually. That is more than the combined profits of the Mag 7 in 2025. And if GPUs do tend to have lower useful lives than this five-year depreciation schedule, future costs for GPU upgrades are significantly being understated.

Conclusion:

My view is the AI narrative is dependent on answers to the four questions highlighted above being resolved positively. I view this as unlikely. This is likely to trigger at least a few "hiccups" for the AI revolution in 2026. And given how dependent equities have been on the AI narrative for their gains over the past three years, any headwinds on the AI front could put the overall market in jeopardy. Or at least vulnerable to a significant correction if not an outright bear market in the year ahead.

data.title: HTD: Tax-Advantaged Monthly Income From Dividends
data.description:
I rate John Hancock Tax-Advantaged Dividend Income Fund (HTD) a Buy, for income focused investors who are looking for long term buy and hold investments. The closed-end fund, or CEF, currently trades at a -4.74% discount to NAV, paying an 7.74% yield on a forward basis after raising the monthly distribution by 14.5% in July 2025. Holdings are a combination of preferred and common securities in the high-dividend paying utilities sector.

The most important factors that influence my opinion of this CEF are:

  • Trades at a discount of -4.74% to net asset value (NAV)
  • Performs long term: 5-year total return of 69.45%, and 10 year 168.75%.
  • Currently pays a fixed monthly distribution of $0.158, which yields 7.74% annually.
  • Goes ex-dividend on 12/11.
  • Tax-advantaged dividends that qualify for the lower, long-term capital gains rate.
  • Distributions comprised of dividends, long and short-term cap gains, and return of capital.
  • Uses 31.74% leverage.

Full disclosure, I own shares in HTD as part of my income portfolio. What attracted me to the fund was the consistent monthly distributions, equity dividend focus, and the total returns. Since it is made up of 125 equities, the fund has the ability to pay income from dividends and capital gains when needed, while continuing to appreciate long term. The tax advantaged element is also attractive, as the dividends will be taxed at the more favorable long term capital gains rate. I also liked the fact that it diversified my portfolio of REITs (such as STWD, ARI), MLPs (EIPI), BDCs (ARCC), option income (QQQI, SPYI, IWMI, BTCI) and bond funds (PDI, PDO).

Seeking Alpha Quant does not cover the fund. Year to date, the fund is up +11% on price as seen in the chart below.

Tax-Advantaged Dividend Income Fund

The fund’s investment objective is to provide a high level of after-tax total return from dividend income and capital appreciation. It will invest at least 80% in dividend paying securities including common and preferred stock. The distributions are managed to include qualified dividend income meaning the stock is held for over a year therefore the dividend is taxed at the lower long-term rate.

Currently the fund has total net assets of $911.52 million, with managed assets of $1.35 billion. Its Inception date was 2/27/2004.

The fund is primarily made up of utilities (45%) and financials (33%).

The top 10 holdings in the fund are established companies that pay larger dividends such as American Electric Power (AEP), Kinder Morgan (KMI), Duke Energy (DUK), and Verizon (VZ). Verizon, for example, currently pays a 6.69% yield.

Performance To Date

An investment of $10,000 over 10 years in HTD would be worth $25,780, with dividends reinvested. If an investor took the distributions as income, the price of the stock would have increased from a cost basis of $10K to a market value of $11,900. In my opinion, the trade off for a lower distribution rate at 8% vs say a residential mortgage REIT at over 10% is worth it. For a long-term investor concerned about principal erosion, this CEF is paying out a decent monthly payment, and the price of the fund is holding steady, possibly growing. So if an investor needed to sell, they could get their money back and more. If the fund drops in price, such as the 2020 Covid crisis, its just a matter of time before the value comes back.

When compared to a sector index using the same $10k over 10 years, HTD outperforms the SPDR Portfolio S&P 500 High Dividend ETF (SPYD) by $2,080.

Valuation

HTD is currently trading at a -4.89% discount to NAV, as of 12/5/25. Historically, the discount has gone below 10% a few times but rarely trades at a premium. While a 10% discount is the best time to buy, the current discount is on average still within reason, in my view.

Over the past 10 years, HTD has increased and lowered the total distribution rate.

The fund normally keeps the monthly distribution rate about the same, increases it every so often, but then pays special dividends, which create the lumpiness over the years. For example, over the past 10 years, HTD has paid on average $0.138 each month with special dividends paid in December.

Competition

In a comparison to similar funds I’ve chosen below, HTD performs in the middle. While its not completely apples to apples, since each fund has a slightly different focus, it helps to show the characteristics of these type of funds. HTD currently has an acceptable distribution yield of almost 8%, with strong performance on 5- and 10-year returns (69%/158%), and a fairly low turnover rate of 21%. At a market cap of $912 million, its just under the billion-dollar Eaton Vance funds.

Eaton Vance Tax-Advantaged Dividend Income Fund (EVT) is the largest fund amongst peers with the top dividend yield at 8.1%. Full disclosure, I own shares in EVT as one of my largest holding in an income portfolio. The stocks held within the fund are different to HTD, hence holding both provides dividend income from multiple sources.

Eaton Vance Enhanced Equity Income Fund II (EOS) stands out with the highest 5-year total return of 63%, and the 10-year total return of 255%. At $1.3 billion in market cap, it’s a well-known fund with outstanding performance. I also invest in EOS, however, notice it is an equity fund, so the distributions are generated from capital gains vs. dividends.

John Hancock Premium Dividend Fund (PDT) has about the same distribution yield of 7.7% but lower 5- and 10-year total returns. It currently trades at an -8.15% discount to NAV making it also attractive.

Eaton Vance Tax-Advantaged Global Dividend Opportunities Fund (ETO) – this is another fund to consider with a slightly different strategy. Globally focused, its dividend yield is 7.1% with the second strongest 10-year price performance of all peers. Note, the turnover is higher in this fund as they seek the best opportunities.

In the chart below, each fund shows performance over 10 years on a total return basis. EOS stands out as the clear winner with a 255% gain, followed by ETO with 201%, and EVT with 175%.

Outlook

Going into 2026, the future for HTD, which is composed of mostly utility and financial stocks is positive in my view. The U.S. economy is currently very healthy, with limited recession risk. Unemployment is at 4.4% and inflation, as measured by the CPI is close to 3%. The utility and financial sectors are performing well and are expected to continue to outperform in the next few years. The December Fed meeting is expected to cut another 25 basis points making closed end funds with distributions around 8% very attractive vs. bond funds, which will fall in yield.

Risks To Rating

The risk to my rating on HTD is that the discount narrows over the next few days as we get closer to a Fed meeting, making the yield less attractive. For those who buy or who own shares, many analysts are calling for a stock market correction caused by inflated valuations on AI stocks. This would cause the CEF to drop in price. The fund uses leverage, which at 31.74% magnifies losses during market drops.

Depending on how an investor wants to use the fund, one of the risks is that the fund is unlikely to match the performance of the S&P 500 (SP500) over time. For example, an investor that won’t need income for the next 10 years would be better off buying an index fund such as SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY) for capital appreciation.

Bottom Line

I rate the John Hancock Tax-Advantaged Dividend Income Fund: HTD - a Buy since it is trading at a -4.74% discount to NAV, paying a monthly distribution yielding almost 8% annually. The closed-end fund is ideal for monthly income and is expected to appreciate in price over time as well. The holdings of the fund are made up of equities primarily in utilities and financials, paying qualified dividends which have the more favorable long term capital gains tax rate. The monthly distributions are very consistent, with special dividends added in December depending on the year. HTD will go ex-dividend on December 11th.

A similar fund to consider is Eaton Vance Tax-Advantaged Dividend Income Fund – EVT, which pays an 8% yield however holds a different basket of equities, which could be considered a good pairing to HTD.

data.title: Axon: Market Overreaction Creates A Buying Opportunity For An Exceptional Business
data.description:
Axon Enterprise, Inc. (AXON) is a company I have admired for quite some time. This is a growing business excelling in its niche market and its founder continues to lead this company in the right direction. I’ve been reluctant to buy shares because of the company’s high valuation but after the company’s recent selloff, I’m adding this company to my portfolio.

Despite underperforming the market year-to-date and having the stock price fall dramatically after the company’s Q3 earnings miss I remain convinced this will be an outperforming stock in the long-term.

Let’s dig into Axon and the company’s recent financial results as I explain why long-term investors should look at this dip as a buying opportunity.

The Company

Axon was founded by Rick Smith back in 1993 after two of Smith’s friends were killed in a road rage incident. Smith believed that through the use of technology, he could help make the world a safer place and thus Axon Enterprise was born. The company’s mission is to protect life. This global leader in public safety has an ambitious goal to reduce all related gun deaths between police and the public in the United States by 50% by 2033.

The company is made of two reportable segments: Software and Sensors and TASER. Products in the Software and Sensors segment include sensors, body cameras, in-car cameras and numerous other sensors and associated software. The TASER segment includes conducted energy devices (“CEDs”) virtual reality training, and so much more.

Axon has built an ecosystem in which they support their customers with training, while in the field (communicate and act) and lastly when it’s time to analyze data and create reports as you can see below:

With a net revenue retention rate of 124%, a net promoter score of greater than 60, and with 95% of revenue tied to subscription plans, it’s clear to see Axon has beloved products which are very sticky. This makes switching from Axon much more difficult and as Axon continues to build out their portfolio, the expanded optionality makes the investment case for this business even more compelling.

Speaking of new opportunities, let’s discuss Axon’s total addressable market and where the business can go from here.

TAM and Future Opportunities

Axon believes they have a total addressable market of $159 billion. As you can see from the graphic below, Axon sees the largest potential in international markets, with enterprises and with domestic state and local governments:

Axon believes they have multiple growth drivers that can continue to drive the business forward. These growth drivers include product upgrades, creating new categories, modernizing old technologies, solving universal problems, and expanding globally.

On the company’s earnings call, Axon 911 was a major emphasis, as Axon’s management team believes this solution solves the problems noted above, like creating a new product category and modernizing outdated technology. Axon 911 focuses heavily on artificial intelligence. Axon recently acquired two companies to help support this Axon 911 initiative. In October 2025, Axon purchased Invictus Apps, Inc. (“Prepared”) for $637.5 million, and in November 2025, the company purchased Carbyne Ltd. (“Carbyne”) for $625 million. Both companies support emergency communications.

Here is what CEO Rick Smith had to say on the Q3 earnings call about the two acquisitions and how they are transforming the 911 process:

“Both leverage AI natively to rethink what's possible. Let me bring this to life. Imagine you need help. You dial 911. Today, the operator listens, gathers information and frenetically types it into a command line system. That information is then passed via a typed message over to a dispatcher who's a different person who reads that information and then relays it over the radio to police officers. It's an inherently inefficient system of the old telephone game, prone to risks of miscommunication. We can turn that process, and we are now turning it on its head. When you make that call, a network of connected devices and software across your city will activate instantly, analyzing information, prompting decisions and allowing the human operator to coordinate a faster, more informed response. Call handlers will focus on helping you instead of typing feverishly, while real-time prompts will enable the dispatch of human or drone responders.”

This certainly sounds like Axon 911 could be an exceptional endeavor that not only brings about further opportunity for the business but also saves more lives in the process.

Additionally, as you can see below, domestic public safety spending has continued to grow over the last few decades, and it’s tough to believe this trend will reverse any time soon:

I believe international growth can be a significant driver moving forward, as well as revenues from outside of the United States now accounting for 16% of total revenue YTD compared to 14% for the nine months ended September 30, 2024. I’d expect this percentage to increase as Axon continues to release new offerings in the years to come.

Lastly, I’d also argue M&A is an additional avenue through which Axon can continue to expand and enter new markets. That’s clearly the plan with Axon 911, given the Prepared and Carbyne acquisitions. Axon has also made some other notable acquisitions with Fusus and Dedrone in 2024. I would expect this to continue as the business folds in more upstart businesses into their ecosystem. When speaking about such acquisitions, here is what COO and CFO, Brittany Bagley had to say about the company’s M&A strategy:

“As we think about our acquisitions, I think what you're seeing is we are investing in companies that tend to be a little bit earlier stage. So we're not really buying revenue. We're not buying an existing business. We're buying an opportunity, and then we're pulling it into our ecosystem and integrating it and really using that to accelerate the opportunity.”

While M&A can work out wonderfully, if poorly executed, they certainly can impact the business’s bottom line, which I’ll discuss next.

Risks

There are numerous risks for this business, which the company outlines in the Risk Factors section of their most recent 10K filing. As I previously mentioned, unsuccessful acquisitions can lead to additional implementation risks and impairment costs, which could materially impact Axon’s financial results.

Product liability and negative brand sentiment could also seriously impact Axon’s brand image should a product malfunction or cause a serious injury. Such an issue could result in lawsuits and loss of brand confidence, which could ultimately lead to a loss in market share.

Axon also faces various government regulations. Complying with both domestic and international laws could result in additional compliance costs and perhaps fees or fines if the company’s products don’t meet such regulations. For instance, given Europe’s much tougher tech laws, I could see some push-back on AI usage which might hinder international adoption or result in product alterations in different markets.

Lastly, I do think there is a risk of competitors and emerging technologies. Technology is changing rapidly and Axon must keep up. However, I do believe Smith and Axon’s management team are committed to allocating capital towards innovation. Given Axon has created a comprehensive ecosystem, I think this is a very sticky business and feel customers will stay with Axon as long as the company can continue to deliver new products and services as they’ve done in the past.

Management

As previously mentioned, Rick Smith is the founder and current CEO of Axon. Smith is a rare visionary who has dedicated his life to protecting life and improving public safety. In 2019, Smith published, “The End of Killing” in which he argued killing was due to technology problems and he offered a roadmap to solve these unnecessary deaths. Smith has a degree from Harvard and an MBA from the University of Chicago.

The company’s Chief Operating Officer and Chief Financial Officer is Brittany Bagley. Prior to joining Axon, Bagley was the Chief Financial Officer at Sonos (SONO).

I am a big believer that culture can play a factor in the long-term results for a company and research has proven the best companies to work for have beaten the overall market. The Glassdoor reviews for Axon are excellent with the majority of employees stating they would recommend it to a friend and employees certainly approve of Smith as you can see below:

I particularly am fond of companies where founders have skin in the game because the founders care more about the organization, thus they are able to maintain their long-term vision which leads to market-beating returns. As noted in Axon’s most recent proxy file, Smith holds just less than 4% of all shares beneficially owned which is a great sign.

Financials

Before we dive into the most recent quarter, let’s zoom out and look at the company’s success over the last few years. Axon has been an exceptional growth story with consistent revenue growth north of 25% over the last few years and adjusted gross margins consistently above 60%, as you can see below:

This has resulted in stellar revenue as you can see below with 31% YoY revenue growth and a CAGR of 34% over the last few years:

In Q3 2025, Axon generated revenue of $711 million. Software and Services generated revenue of $305 million which is an increase of 41% compared to Q3 2024. Connected Devices generated revenue of roughly $405 million which is a 24% increase compared to the prior year third quarter. Breaking down Connected Devices, TASER brought in revenue of $238 million, Personal Sensors brought in revenue of $107 million and Platform Solutions generated revenue of $61 million. These are respective YoY revenue increases of 17%, 20%, and 71%.

Furthermore Axon had a net revenue retention percentage of 124% and have continued to grow both future contract bookings and annual recurring revenue for the quarter as you can see below:

Management raised revenue guidance for the rest of the fiscal year. For Q4 2025, management anticipates revenue in the range of $750 to $755 million implying full year revenue of approximately $2.74 billion, which is 31% growth (prior estimate was $2.65 to $2.73 billion). Management expects adjusted EBITDA for Q4 of $178 to $182 million.

What the market didn’t like was the earnings miss as the company had significantly more operating expenses this quarter compared to Q3 2024 and this resulted in a loss from operations and a net loss for the quarter as you can see below:

Management noted this quarter adjusted gross margin fell by 50 basis points compared to Q3 2024 due primarily from tariffs. Bagley had some commentary on this gross margin decline during the call stating,

“So all of the impact from tariffs is obviously hitting the Connected Devices business overall. This was the first quarter that we had a full quarter of impact from tariffs. So as we look at the year-over-year step down, that really is attributable to tariffs. As long as tariffs stay in place, I view that as sort of a onetime adjustment. So now that's baked into the gross margins. Obviously, as our software business continues to outpace from a growth standpoint, like we talked about, that becomes even at bigger dollars, that becomes a smaller and smaller percentage of our overall gross margin.”

Considering this is impacting Connected Devices which isn’t growing at the rate of Software and Services and it appears to be more of a one-off given the tariff situation, I believe the market overreacted to this news. I don’t foresee this as a significant long-term concern investors should be worried about.

Axon has a solid balance sheet with enough cash and cash equivalents for potential M&A opportunities, plus the business has more current assets than current liabilities, which is positive. The company has acquired more long-term debt this year compared to the balance as of December 31, 2024 (as you can see below), but this was necessary to help fund M&A activity and continued R&D:

Valuation

Axon has a valuation grade of "F" at Seeking Alpha Quant:

However you choose to view Axon, most would still view it as rather expensive even after the Q3 earnings drop. Considering Axon is still growing considerably, I think Prices to sales or EV/Sales are the better valuation metrics. You can see above Axon's P/S and EV/Sales are significantly higher than the sector median. However, these metrics have declined and are much closer to the company's historical five year averages. Below, you can see Axon's forward P/S ratio has come down and is nearing the April lows for this year:

From another perspective, I've created a reverse discounted cash flow model for Axon. Considering Axon's usage of cash this year for some significant acquisitions this year, I think it's more appropriate to use the December 31, 2024 free cash flow value of $330 million, which I'd argue is more appropriate given the business's ample growth opportunities. Using that figure with a discount rate of 10% and a terminal growth rate of 4%, the business would need to continue to grow by 31.5% annually to justify today’s current stock price:

While 31.5% sounds lofty, analysts do expect Axon to grow at a high double-digit rate. Given Axon has grown at a CAGR of 34% over the last few years, 31.5% seems achievable given the numerous expansion opportunities coupled with AI tailwinds.

Wall Street also views Axon as a good investment at the moment with most analysts stating the business is a "Strong Buy" or "Buy" and have an average target of $822.50, which implies significant upside as you can see below:

Conclusion

Axon has been on my watch-list for some time now and I’m glad I was able to initiate a position in this fantastic founder-led business. The company continues to grow at a stellar pace and has numerous pathways in which to expand. Axon’s 911 opportunity seems especially promising with the additions of the Prepared and Carbyne to the Axon ecosystem.

This is a sticky business with a net revenue retention rate of 124% and ARR and future contracted bookings continue to grow.

To me, the market considerably overreacted to this earnings miss and while Axon isn’t exactly cheap from a valuation perspective, no top-notch company ever is, which is why I view this decline as a gift to long-term growth oriented investors. I’ve initiated a position and will continue to add shares should the stock continue to decline even more.

data.title: AIRR: Industrial ETF Benefiting From Reshoring
data.description:
This article updates my review of July 2024 in light of current holdings and recent performance.

Geopolitics, supply chain resilience, and U.S. government incentives have bolstered reshoring in 2025. First Trust RBA American Industrial RenaissanceTM ETF (AIRR) is one of the ETFs that have benefited, and may continue to benefit, from this trend.

AIRR Strategy

AIRR is a thematic fund launched on 3/10/2014 tracking the Richard Bernstein Advisors American Industrial Renaissance® Index. AIRR has a portfolio of 52 stocks, a trailing 12-month yield of 0.21% and a total expense ratio of 0.70%.

The fund focuses on the domestic economy through small and mid-cap companies of the industrial sector, and to a lesser extent of the banking industry. As described by First Trust, eligible companies must be in the Russell 2500 Index and directly related to manufacturing, infrastructure, or banking. They must have non-U.S. sales inferior to 25% and positive 12-month forward earnings estimates. Bank stocks are selected in states of traditional manufacturing hubs, and their aggregate weight is limited to approximately 10% of asset value. The index is rebalanced on a quarterly basis, with a maximum limit of 4% in any constituent. The portfolio turnover rate was 75% in the most recent fiscal year and 48% in the previous year.

Portfolio

The fund is exclusively invested in U.S. companies, with 79% of asset value in small and micro caps and 21% in mid-caps. Sector-wise, 91.5% of assets is in industrials and 8.5% in banks. Due to the portfolio composition, I will use as a benchmark the S&P SmallCap 600® Capped Industrials Index, represented by Invesco S&P SmallCap Industrials ETF (PSCI).

The portfolio has a focus on construction & engineering (30.9%) with also notable exposure in aerospace & defense (13.7%) and building products (10.6%). Other industries are below 9%. Compared to PSCI, AIRR overweights mostly construction & engineering, aerospace & defense and banks. It significantly downplays machinery, commercial and professional services.

The portfolio has low to moderate company risk. The top 10 holdings, listed in the next table, represent 36% of asset value and the heaviest position weighs 4.4%.
