data.title: Orange S.A.: Superb Returns - Time To Leave (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
I believe it's fair to say you'll have a hard time finding someone more consistently bullish on undervalued telcos like Orange S.A. (OTC:ORANY, Euronext Paris: ORA) than I have been. For years, have been speaking to the appeal of this company and what it may, might, and would likely achieve. I know many have followed me on the investment, and have, like me, been able to realize returns of over 60-70% inclusive of dividends. Look at both this article here, which is my latest article, but also the January 2025 article, which has produced the returns you see below.

Value investing is hard. People believe it, or consider it to be easy. It's not. The tricky part is separating the value investments from the value traps, a practice I still get wrong from time to time, despite over a decade of experience in professional investing and analytics (meaning I make my main living off of it). There's also the simple fact that it's very easy to, in retrospect, say something like that the company you see above, is appealing - but that wasn't the view most analysts held at the time when I invested.

Value investing takes going against the grain with confidence, ignoring 80-95% of the rest of the market, and doing your own due diligence and research and not relying on others, unless you find it consistently and objectively solid. It takes a lot more work - and it takes forecasting the potential for developments like the one below.

It's fair to say, in my view, that it requires you to take the reins of your own investments - firmly - and make decisions. Unlike with approaches where you follow trends and the "hot new thing," you're essentially trying to do the opposite. If the general market is a train on tracks, I view most investors as being on that train and track, following along wherever it may go. I view myself as being outside of the train, picking through what I can find to find "good things," while also trying to foresee where exactly that train may go, instead of just looking at "how fast it goes."

A rough metaphor, but it works for me.

Value investing also, and this is where I differ from other writers on SA, requires you to be able to say "This is my target, and this is now enough." Then we rotate.

That's where we are with Orange, and have been for over 6 months.

Orange: Returns going forward are likely anamic - I say it's time to consider rotation

As I said, Orange has been a Hold for over 6 months now. If you want to follow my approach, you can't wait for me to give rating changes. Unless it's something I specifically look at, which I do for some companies, it might be some time before I get around to the investment in question.

You need to instead look at the targets I gave. My price target, or PT, for Orange was €14.5/share, meaning when it's close to or above that, the company literally becomes a Hold at that second. Because of how swiftly the company rose, my alert was literally set to that - and when it hit, I sold. This was in late August.

I no longer hold Orange common shares. I realized a 70%+ profit, inclusive of FX and RoR, for some portions of my position, annualized above 150%+ for the investment. That's how I invest. No position is "sacred." I'm here to make money, and I will make money where I see it possible. I saw that possibility in Orange - I no longer consider that possibility or potential to be high.

Simply put, Orange has realized all of the upside the company had going for it about 2 quarters ago. The Q3 '25 increased guidance targets in terms of EBITDAaL do not constitute reasons for me to increase my PT - all of this was already included in my original assumptions. Even if it wasn't, a 50 bps bump is hardly enough for a fundamental rating change. Most forward-looking valuation-oriented analysts that I know had a target for Orange between €14-€15/share. I view anywhere between those two marks as "valid" - but not above.

Why not above?

There's a complex political risk remaining in Orange. The potential acquisition of SFR with Bouygues and Iliad would consolidate the market, but the implications of this are still somewhat unclear, given a high multiple for the proposed purchase. For Orange, that sale would be a 27% stake in Altice, mostly B2C assets, and this is the smallest market share of the three (given Orange's larger size in turn, as well as its market share). The specifics here are, as I said, complex. That's why I believe there's risk here.

The Spanish MasMovil merger/solution was something I included in my original forecasts. I won't add to Orange for it - cost side synergies are significant, but beyond this, small changes are expected. Switching costs remain low in Spain, and it's one of the trickier telco markets in all of Europe.

What good parts remain on the upside for Orange that I consider not included in my original expectations are upside potentials in its growth markets - Africa and the Middle East. But here, in its 12 markets, the visibility remains low, and the overall share of profits compared to the total does not constitute a high enough part of the company's sales mix to really make a big difference or justify a higher upside.

I have been saying for years that Orange is the premier operator in France, with a good yield. I was able to get over 7% yield for the lowest-cost portion of my position. All of that upside and yield is now gone. The yield is less than 5.5% (which means there's more yield in others), and growth upside, even considering the above-mentioned mergers and upsides, does not in any scenario justify a higher AEPS than €1.25/share in my book - not even in 2-3 years.

It's not just Orange that has grown, by the way. Telefonica (TEF) is also no longer an attractive investment.

Beyond that, risks for Orange remain political. It's a French company, which means that any reductions in cost that are related to personnel or even AI are going to face regulatory and political opposition in France. And with the combination of political risks, the lack of visibility in growth markets.

Allow me for a moment to return to my "train metaphor." If you listened or followed my investment when it was cheap, you're part of the category of investors that watch the train go and consider where it goes, while looking at (or listening to others finding) upsides and returns "outside" of the norm/the "train" where most of the market is. If you, on the other hand, are looking to buy now, like the Quant and Wall Street recommendations are saying, chances are higher (in my view) that you're on that train.

Being on that train is not necessarily a negative. I've missed out on tons of return opportunities by being unwilling to listen to where "things might go" in companies or investments where I don't view the upsides or the fundamentals as clear enough. At this point, I consider myself close to immune from that type of exuberance. But being on that train is a diametrically opposed approach to the one that I use. It's based on things, especially now with the AI bubble, like TAM, market potential, and replacement potentials, without any grounding in what's happened before.

It doesn't make that approach, or those potential returns, invalid or impossible - but to me it's an approach that's alien, because I would not want to expose my hard-earned money that way.

And because of this, despite good returns and upside in Q3 '25 for Orange, I consider the company unattractively valued for my approach based on what you see below.

Valuation for Orange - Now no longer attractive

Orange was attractive when a 10-12x forward P/E saw a 15-25% annualized rate of return with conservative growth forecasts. We currently have a scenario where a 12x P/E, even at a normalized valuation of 19x P/E, generates no higher than an 11.5% annualized RoR - and that's with a 5%+ dividend yield. While some may call this attractive, I am not one of them.

The reasons are several. First of all, Orange has a historically notorious high failure rate for forecasts. Telco forecasts are supposed to be stable and dependable. Orange is the opposite. It negatively fails its forecast targets more than 70% of the time on a 1-year basis with a 10% margin of error. That's strike one. Strike two is not only forecast accuracy, but the likelihood of the growth forecast you see above. I do not see it as likely enough to even consider that ~€1.25/share in 2027E valid at this time. At this point, it's still a bit of a pipe dream.

That doesn't invalidate the solid fundamentals that Orange has. But whenever you have a company that's swung up as this one has, you need to also consider the potential for that to go down just as quickly. In that scenario, I'd have lost a profit of over 70% - and by the time I sold, I'd already reinvested that into companies that are up 19% today, compared to Orange, which, since my sale, is down from my sales price.

Valuation, dear readers, matters - and Orange no longer can be said to be attractive enough here.

Alternatives for Orange are other telcos - and these are not cheap either. Telefonica is, at best, fairly valued. Vodafone (VOD) is really the only company that can be argued to be attractive here, and even that is no longer as attractive any longer either. In fact, about a week ago, I sold my stake in Vodafone as well based on anemic return possibilities.

For Orange, I'm lowering my mid-range PT of €14.5/share to €14/share, with a fair value of €15.5. What I mean by this is that the company could go to €15.5/share without issue. It's also potentially buyable here, if you consider that return to be good enough. 11% per year isn't bad.

The reason that I don't consider it good enough is a combination of forecast inaccuracies, political risks, and above all, the overall market risk. Say we were in a market where we weren't seeing the worst value fundamentals since before the dot-com bubble or the GFC, or even the great depression. Orange might be attractive.

But because we're in a situation where every value-related KPI is telling me to be careful, I'm no longer willing to hold this.

I say Hold, and I say €14/share in PT.

Risks are as follows.

Risks & Considerations

The risks for Orange are mostly valuation and macro-related, aside from its political risks. The company still technically has a double-digit upside at this price, but with the combination of the broader market valuations, a near-20x P/E on a normalized basis for current results, and with a lower than 5.5% yield, I believe my money is better served sitting in other investments, or simply sitting in a MMF or interest investment with zero or close to zero risk.

My thesis for the company is updated for April 2024, as follows, and the ADR ORANY, a 1:1 ADR, comes to a PT updated of $16.32.

Thesis

Orange was one of the more appealing telcos in terms of valuation in Europe, but that upside is now gone, not just from Orange, but from many other telcos as well.

Because of broader market valuations and the company's individual risk profile in terms of forecast accuracy and political risk, I view this company as, for the most part, unattractive at this time.

Orange remains a Hold here as of December 2025 and after Q3 '25 results. A price target that I would consider attractive for investment based on my goals would be around €14/share - though every investor, of course, needs to look at their own targets, goals, and strategies. I would also always consult a finance professional before making investment decisions such as this. I have raised my PT for the company as of this article.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside based on earnings growth or multiple expansions/reversions.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators, and 0 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 3/5 "stars" here, which typically isn't enough. Based on this, and a valuation target of €140/share where I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me. I give the company a Hold rating as of December 2025.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Amazon: Resilient Consumers, Reasonable Capex Outlook, And Flat Valuation Justify A Buy
data.description:
What Can We Learn From The Holiday Season?

We are currently in the holiday season, and the Black Friday sales can give us a clear picture on whether U.S. consumers are still in good shape. According to Mastercard SpendingPulse, U.S. retail sales excluding autos were up 4.1% YoY on Black Friday, showing growth acceleration from 3.4% YoY in CY2024. Online retail sales grew 10.4% YoY, down from 14.6% YoY a year ago. Despite lingering cost pressure from tariffs and a higher employment rate, I'm surprised that U.S. consumers are still resilient, which is positive to Amazon.com, Inc.'s (NASDAQ:AMZN) retail business.

Turning to the job market, November private payrolls unexpectedly dropped by 32,000, driven by professional and business services. AMZN has also been cutting its workforce this year, due to the large adoption of AI tools and agents. According to Reuters, the company announced its largest job cut since 2022 in October, slashing roughly 10% of its corporate employees. I believe that these layoffs reflect improved operating efficiency rather than macro deterioration.

In my last analysis after Q1 FY2025, I maintained a Buy rating during the April market selloff, arguing that AMZN was able to navigate tariff impact through AI automation and strong pricing power. At that time, I was concerned that demand might have been pulled forward, adding more growth pressure in 2H CY2025. However, the U.S. economy proved resilient, and AI demand becomes even stronger than 1H. We can see that AMZN's online sales accelerated in Q2 and Q3, and AWS revenue growth is also trending higher.

Overall, I think this holiday season does not indicate slowing U.S. consumption. The market consensus also suggests that AI demand will remain robust in 2026, with top hyperscalers further boosting capex next year. AMZN currently trades at 33x non-GAAP P/E fwd, similar to Microsoft (MSFT) and Google (GOOGL). So its valuation still looks reasonable, deserving a Buy.

AWS: Double Capacity By FY2027

In early November, the company signed a $38 billion multi-year deal with OpenAI to leverage AWS infrastructure, which should keep its growth above +20% in FY2026. So far, AWS remains the cloud market leader by revenue, outpacing Azure and Google Cloud. According to the statement, the OpenAI partnership is expected to fully deploy all contracted capacity by the end of next year, which explains why its FY2026 capex would be even higher (higher capex is on a dollar basis, though I expect the YoY growth to slow from FY2025 levels).

On the recent earnings call, management said, "we're now double the power capacity that AWS was in 2022, and we're on track to double again by 2027."

AWS saw a major inflection back in FY2024. As shown in the chart, the revenue growth started to accelerate in Q1 FY2024 and reached 20.2% YoY in the last quarter. We also saw a strong expansion in EBIT margin, driven by improved operating efficiency (including corporate layoffs). I expect AWS growth to accelerate further into FY2026 as current AI demand remains strong.

Trainium3 Offers 40% Better Performance

This week, AMZN announced its next-gen AI chip Trainium3, built on 3nm process. So far, Trainium adoptions are still limited, with only a small number of large customers, as noted on the Q3 earnings call. Management indicated that Trainium2 already achieved multi-billion dollar revenue, up 150% QoQ.

According to the announcement, Trainium3 would outperform Trainium2 by 40% in computing power, energy efficiency, and memory bandwidth. Management expects more demand for Trainium3 when it becomes broadly available in early 2026, attracting more medium size customers due to its lower cost benefit.

Meanwhile, AMZN continues to work closely with Nvidia (NVDA) for its most advanced AI chips. Over the long run, however, I believe the company plans to reduce dependence on third-party chips for both cost and competitive reasons. Therefore, while Trainium is not a major revenue driver today, it's growing rapidly and could become a growth catalyst in the future.

$125 Billion In FY2025 Capex, With More To Come In FY2026

AMZN's AI infrastructure spending has remained elevated. Like other hyperscalers, the company is rushing to stay competitive in the current AI race. Its capex as a percentage of total revenue has been climbing higher since Q1 FY2024 and jumped to 19.6% in Q3 FY2025, as shown on the chart. This ratio tells us that the company is more willing to allocate a larger portion of revenue toward AI investment. This substantial increase is consistent with other AI players in the Magnificent 7, suggesting that AI demand has continued to accelerate since this summer, beating market estimates.

For example, Microsoft's capex to revenue ratio increased to 44.9% in the latest quarter, up from 30.9% sequentially. META's ratio also surged to 37.8%, while Google's remained stable at 23.4%. However, on a YoY basis, capex of these three companies has jumped 70% to 100% in their last quarters.

Management guided to a $125 billion capex outlook for FY2025. Based on the Q4 FY2025 revenue guidance, we can calculate its capex growth would moderate to 17.5% YoY, with the capex to revenue ratio of 15.4%. However, they indicated that capex would continue to increase in FY2026.

AMZN has barely generated FCF since the beginning of FY2025. On a TTM basis, its FCF is down 79% YoY. This is common across hyperscalers, as all of them are aggressively expanding their AI infrastructures this year. Based on my Q4 capex to revenue ratio, AMZN does not appear to be "overspending" compared to MSFT and META.

Why Has P/E Come Down So Much?

According to Seeking Alpha's valuation metrics, AMZN's non-GAAP P/E fwd is 33.3x, 80% below its 5-year average. This reflects a normalization process, driven by improved operating efficiency. Operating margin jumped from 2.4% in FY2022 to an expected 11.3% in FY2025 (based on the high end of 4Q guidance). As mentioned earlier, corporate layoffs have helped keep General and Administrative expenses nearly flat since FY2022.

AMZN traded around 30x during the April pullback. Since then, the multiple has expanded modestly to 33x, which means that the stock is not in an AI bubble.

As shown in the chart, we haven't seen major pickup in AMZN's other multiples over the past 12 months. I believe the stock should trade at a slightly higher multiple to better reflect its AI growth potential.

Conclusion

In summary, AMZN remains a secular AI growth story. AWS revenue growth could accelerate above 20% into FY2026, supported by strong AI demand and the OpenAI partnership, which is expected to fully utilize AWS capacity by the end of next year. Meanwhile, AWS continues to expand its infrastructure, with capacity expected to double by FY2027. The scaling of AI deployment, combined with workforce optimization, should improve margins. Despite the higher capex outlook next year, I don't see signs of overspending, assuming that current AI demand is sustainable. At 33x non-GAAP P/E fwd, AMZN's valuation still attractive. Therefore, it's still a solid buy.

data.title: Sell Upstart And SoFi: It's The Defaults, Not The Interest Rates
data.description:
My sorry track record to date on Sofi (SOFI) and Upstart (UPST)

I wrote several articles over the past few years suggesting that investors sell consumer lender stocks SoFi (SOFI) and Upstart (UPST). The Upstart call (this past June 4) hasn’t been bad; the stock has fallen by 9%. But SoFi? My goodness. Since my last article (March 12, 2024), the stock has tripled!

Why my negativity? Three of the key reasons were:

  1. Both of the companies are currently primarily unsecured consumer lenders. This product has been mature for several decades, meaning that the long-term growth trend for their product will be in the low single digits.
  2. They have plenty of competition, from the traditional credit card lenders (Capital One, JPMorgan Chase, American Express) to a large group of new fintechs. I personally get mail from these companies 2-4 times a week.
  3. Since they are start-ups, neither company has been tested in a tough economic environment.

While my track record says I should keep my thoughts to myself, I think of those baseball pitchers who get rocked for 6 runs but go out the following week and take the ball again. Upstart and SoFi’s very recent stock market performance convinced me to take the ball again.

Last week’s news.

During Thanksgiving week, Upstart’s stock rose by 19% and SoFi’s by 14%. What was the good news? Increased confidence that the Federal Reserve is going to cut the fed funds rate at its next session:

“J.P. Morgan said it now expects the U.S. Federal Reserve to deliver a 25-basis-point rate cut in December, reversing its earlier call that policymakers would stay on hold until January.” (Nov 26 Reuters)

Why the increased confidence in a rate cut? The same article gives a clue:

“Goldman Sachs said in a separate note that…‘the September jobs report may have sealed a 25 basis points cut at the December 9-10 FOMC meeting.” (Nov 26 Reuters)

Fed Governor Christopher Waller echoed Goldman’s thoughts about jobs:

“What is that data telling us? First, that the labor market is still weak and near stall speed…” (November 17 speech)

So we have two trends at work – a weaker job market and lower interest rates. Which trend is more important to the stock of a consumer lender? That question is the focus of this article.

Interest rates and the unemployment rate are opposites.

A consumer lender clearly likes both a low unemployment rate and a low fed funds rate. The former because it keeps loan defaults low, the latter because it increases the “interest margin”, which is the spread between the loan yield and the cost of funds that finance the loan. The sharp rise in Upstart and SoFi’s stock prices last week says that investors expect the interest margin widening opportunity without the cost of higher defaults. Does that happen much? This chart says no:

They nearly always move in the opposite direction. At present, with inflation still running above the Fed’s 2% target, it would be surprising to see a much lower fed funds rate unless unemployment is rising. So the Goldilocks scenario of lower interest rates without rising unemployment is unlikely.

Which matters more to consumer lender stocks – interest rates or defaults?

Neither Upstart nor Sofi have been public companies for very long, so I’ll use Capital One as the consumer lender in this experiment. Here is the correlation between Capital One’s stock price and the fed funds rate since the year 2000. Note that I inverted the fed funds rate because lower rates should mean a higher stock price.

There is some correlation, but it’s pretty messy. Now let’s compare Capital One’s stock price to the banking industry’s credit card chargeoff rate, again inverted:

A much better correlation. Note that the stock did far better over the past year than the modest improvement in chargeoffs suggested. That’s because of investor optimism about the earnings benefits of Capital One’s merger with major competitor Discover.

Then what drives the unsecured credit default rate? The unemployment rate seems like the likely candidate.

This chart tells clearly proves that hypothesis:

Only the COVID period was different because the unemployment spike was quite short-lived due to the government pumping $4½ trillion in subsidies into the economy.

Further, defaults drive credit growth

Check out this chart:

When lenders see loan chargeoffs rise, they naturally tighten their lending standards, and vice versa. That is happening already; national unsecured consumer credit growth has been negative YTD, following the COVID bubble.

So where is the unemployment rate heading?

Looks like it is headed higher:

“Hiring by American businesses dropped last month…Private employers shed 32,000 jobs in November, a swing from the 47,000 private-sector jobs that ADP estimated were added in October.” (December 3, CNBC)

“U.S. employers have announced 1,099,500 job cuts through October, an increase of 65% from the first 10 months of 2024.” (November 6, Fox News)

Why the slowdowns? Fox News has some thoughts:

“Some industries are correcting after the hiring boom of the pandemic, but this comes as AI adoption, softening consumer and corporate spending, and rising costs drive belt-tightening and hiring freezes," said Andy Challenger, workplace expert…’Those laid off now are finding it harder to secure new roles, which could further loosen the labor market,’ Challenger added.” (November 6, Fox News)

Then think about the massive investments currently underway in AI, robotics and autonomous driving. All job killers. And Fox News didn’t mention the turmoil caused by the seemingly daily shifts in tariff policy, which is especially hard on small businesses.

The outlook for recent college graduates – a sweet spot for unsecured consumer lenders – looks even worse:

“Job postings on Handshake—a career platform for early-career talent—have fallen more than 16% between August 2024 and August 2025, while the average number of applications per role has jumped 26%. And soon-to-be college graduates already see it coming. More than 60% of the class of 2026 is pessimistic about their career prospects.” (November 19, Fortune)

Summing up – time to sell Upstart and SoFi soon.

Logically, if these job market trends persist or – more likely, in my view – accelerate, then investor attention will eventually shift from lower interest rates to higher unemployment and default rates, which in turn will lead to higher credit expenses and lower loan originations.

Right now, investors are pretty confident – SoFi is selling at 49 times estimated ’26 GAAP EPS. Upstart's valuation is trickier. Wall Street has accepted the company's earnings definition, which differs from GAAP primarily because it excludes as an expense any employee compensation paid in stock. That expense has been about $0.30 a share in recent quarters. I therefore reduce the Upstart's SA '26 Wall Street estimate of $2.40 to $1.20 to get a comparable figure to SoFi and nearly all publicly traded companies. That puts Upstart at a near 40 times P/E.

The high valuations create lots of room on the downside. I therefore say sell these stocks.

data.title: Stitch Fix: Buyer Stabilization And Average Order Growth (Upgrade)
data.description:
As we head into 2026 on the back of a very expensive stock market, small and mid-cap stocks that have been left in the dust over the past year's AI gold rush are looking like increasingly more attractive rebound plays.

Stitch Fix, Inc. (SFIX), in my view, is looking more appealing as a potential rebound candidate after the e-commerce company's latest fiscal Q1 (October quarter) results. Amid a battered retail landscape, Stitch Fix has seemingly stabilized its active customer base and converged its product around a smaller, yet core, customer base that is spending more on its site.

I wrote a Sell article on Stitch Fix in September, when the stock was hovering around $5.60 per share. Recent results for Stitch Fix have been choppy, with the company's original guidance target calling for just 1-5% y/y growth for FY26, falling beneath consensus expectations. However, in Stitch Fix's most recent update, the company noted that it saw strong unexpected demand, which is driving a lift to the full-year outlook. In my view, this is more than supported by Stitch Fix's recent stabilization in customer counts, though it will be key for us to watch performance during the critical holiday quarter. With recent trends improving, I'm resetting my rating on Stitch Fix back to neutral.

In my view, at current share prices, there is a relatively balanced bull and bear case for this stock. On the positive side for this company:

  • Stitch Fix appears to be migrating toward a lower-volume, but higher-spending customer base. Revenue per active customer is growing 5% y/y, despite what many other retail companies have described as a very promotional retail environment in which many customers are trading down. This suggests that Stitch Fix is doing a good job at appealing to higher-income, less elastic consumers that are willing to pay for access to Stitch Fix's stylists and upmarket products (the company's 40%+ gross margins are quite healthy for an apparel company).
  • Cost discipline. Amid slowing growth, we appreciate the fact that Stitch Fix has conducted multiple rounds of layoffs to maintain positive single-digit adjusted EBITDA margins.

At the same time, however, I am concerned about a number of factors:

  • Active client trends have been choppy. Until the bleeding stopped in Q1, Stitch Fix had been consistently shedding customers every quarter, potentially exposing the company as a fad or niche that has stayed past its welcome.
  • Higher advertising spend is eating into margins. The company has also dumped more into brand marketing in order to sustain its current unimpressive pace of growth.

From a valuation perspective, Stitch Fix is neither cheap nor expensive for its growth prospects. At current post-earnings share prices near $5, the stock trades at a market cap of $693.2 million. Netting off the $244.2 million of cash (against zero debt) on the company's latest balance sheet gives us an enterprise value of $449.0 million.

For FY26 (the year for Stitch Fix ending in October 2026), Stitch Fix has updated its full-year revenue guidance to $1.32-$1.35 billion, reflecting a 4.2-6.5% y/y growth range that is substantially better than a 1-5% growth rate expected previously. The company is also now expecting a midpoint $43 million of adjusted EBITDA, or a 3.2% midpoint margin versus 2.9% expected previously.

Against this outlook, Stitch Fix trades at 10.4x EV/FY26 adjusted EBITDA. This isn't an overly expensive EBITDA multiple in the context of a very expensive S&P 500. And yet, we should also consider the fact that Stitch Fix is expending considerable effort and advertising dollars merely to maintain a flattish customer base and achieve mid-single-digit revenue growth. I'm not clear yet on what catalysts the company can leverage in order to create substantial progress on the earnings front.

Post Q1, I'm a little more optimistic on the company's prospects, with its brand showing some level of staying power despite a weaker macro. That said, I still prefer to remain on the sidelines until proof of a more sustained rebound crystallizes.

Q1 Download

Let's now go through Stitch Fix's latest Q1 (October quarter) results in greater detail. The Q1 earnings summary is shown below:

Stitch Fix's revenue grew 7% y/y to $342.1 million, beating Wall Street's expectations of $336.7 million (+6% y/y) by a one-point margin, while also improving substantially from a -3% y/y decline in Q2.

To me, perhaps the most important indicator here is the fact that Stitch Fix has showcased stabilization in its active client base. In Q1, the company counted 2.31 million active clients, which was flat to Q4 (a quarter in which the company had lost 44k clients, and then 22k in the quarter before that).

Meanwhile, average revenue per active client is up 5% y/y to $559, reflecting a customer base that has converged around a smaller core of higher-spending customers. The company notes that its improved selection of higher-end athleisure brands, such as Vuori, has struck a chord with both women and men and sparked a strong fall sales season. Per CEO Matt Baer's remarks on the Q3 earnings call:

"In women's, we saw a strong start to fall sales across key seasonal categories such as sweaters, coats, jackets and vests, which combined grew 19% year-over-year. Sneakers, which were up 63% year-over-year, driven by New Balance, Gola and adidas and Wide leg Denim was up 217%, driven by outsized performance in Dave's Denim, Pistola and Madewell. We've also seen great client responses to new brands, especially within activewear and footwear, such as Varley, Birkenstock and Roan. And we're excited to continue to add new brands to our assortment in the coming months. Our men's business delivered a second consecutive quarter of double-digit revenue growth by leaning more into the elevated everyday and athleisure styles our clients are looking for.

Seasonal categories such as fleece, sweaters and outerwear grew 57% combined, while denim grew 30% and sneakers grew 24% year-over-year. Brands like Travis Matthew and Vuori delivered outsized growth and remain trusted client favorites for style, versatility and quality, while new brands such as Katon, Industry and NN07 have introduced more style and trend into our assortment. We believe that our expanded relevance in activewear and athleisure, footwear and accessories, in particular, could unlock a significant wallet share opportunity and that our fair share with our existing client base in these categories is approximately $1 billion of incremental revenue. We are confident in our ability to capture increased market share in the future."

Still, we're worried about a couple of factors. Active customers are defined rather loosely (a person who has made a purchase within the last 12 months) and have seen choppy trends: the stabilization this quarter isn't yet a true indication that Stitch Fix's niche has found a floor. Second, there are also signs that the holiday season is shaping up to be highly promotional, which will force Stitch Fix to compete. For example, Alo, a newer yoga-oriented brand which together with Vuori is eating into Lululemon's dominance in athleisure, offered 30% off all items for Singles Day, Black Friday, and Cyber Monday (the brand is rarely on sale).

Stitch Fix's gross margins are already facing pressure from tariffs, down -180bps y/y to 43.6%: indicating the company may not have much room to go down further on price.

Furthermore, we note that advertising rose to 9.9% of revenue, 50bps higher from 9.4% in the year-ago quarter: and the net result is still only single digit revenue growth, and flat sequential active customers. Management was able to drive savings from its headcount consolidation, but overall margin pressure pushed adjusted EBITDA margins down to 3.9%, down 30bps y/y, while on a nominal basis adjusted EBITDA of $13.4 million was approximately flat to the prior-year Q1.

Key Takeaways

Stitch Fix's revenue beat in Q1 and what may be a light at the end of the tunnel when it comes to active client churn are the core reasons that the stock rallied post-Q1, and the reason I'm now neutral instead of bearish on the company's prospects. Still, I'm not yet a long-term buyer of this stock, as the company has to prove it can return to meaningful client expansion, while also showcasing a return to positive margin growth. Right now, we're not getting solid indicators on either front, so I prefer to monitor this stock from the sidelines.

data.title: Cellebrite: Solid Setup Going Into Next Year
data.description:
Summary

I previously upgraded Cellebrite DI Ltd. (CLBT) to a "Buy" rating as there were solid developments that painted a strong long-term growth picture. With the bearish narrative on poor federal demand out of the way and platform adoption accelerating, with Inseyets migration progressing well with Cloud/SaaS ARR compounding far faster than the base, I see a solid setup for FY2026.

The U.S. Federal Business Has Pivoted From Headwind To Tailwind

When I upgraded the stock, my core argument was that the long-term growth picture was improving, even though the U.S. federal business was still messy. Q3 lends credence to my case and effectively removes that overhang. Management confirmed that the federal segment returned to y/y growth in Q3, driven by large expansion deals with major agencies. This is literally the datapoint that smashes that bearish narrative, as it shows that the budget delays and whatever procurement friction from 1H25 were timing issues, not signs of demand problems.

Management's tone regarding the demand trend for 2026 is also very bullish, in my view. While they didn’t give a figure, I thought the message was clear. They expect annual recurring revenue (ARR) to accelerate next year, which, I think, is a very fair “guide” since the targeted funding is released and pent-up demand for Inseyets and Unlock is coming through. You add on top the pending FedRAMP High authorization (which now has the Department of Justice as the sponsor); qualitatively, there is a really strong growth outlook ahead.

In any case, investors that have been waiting for confirmation of a federal rebound now have that evidence. The primary bear case (the federal demand slump) is now effectively off the table.

Platform Adoption

Platform adoption was one of the key points I discussed. Of which, I noted Q2 as the inflection point as Inseyets migration went to >40%. The momentum continued, and as of Q3, CLBT exited ~47% of its digital-forensics license base converted to Inseyets, tracking ahead of the 50% goal by year-end. At this pace, I think it is just about confirmed that CLBT will beat that target. Ramping up this adoption figure is important because it effectively lifts the previous growth limiter (staff count growth). With Inseyets, the platform’s growth is no longer tied to license count and staffing levels. With more automation, the idea is agencies can deploy far more licenses than the number of human operators they have, and that effectively opens up a new growth avenue that wasn’t possible before (just imagine every human operator can operate two licenses; that is effectively a double in the addressable market). The lifetime value of customers should also go up as Inseyets becomes the platform for the rest of the cloud and SaaS product suite (with Inseyets, adding modules like Guardian or Pathfinder is much easier).

As a recap, please refer to a quote from my previous post below on the broader impact that comes with Guardian cloud adoption.

To put it simply, instead of just selling a standalone tool for a specific task—like extracting data from a phone—CLBT is now providing the central hub where agencies manage their entire digital investigation. With Guardian, customers are not just buying a product; they are embedding CLBT into their daily operational workflow for managing, sharing, and securing evidence. Once an agency's processes and case data are built around this platform, it becomes incredibly difficult and costly to switch to a competitor. This creates a much stickier relationship and transforms CLBT from a simple vendor into an integrated partner.

What really stands out, though, is the momentum in cloud and SaaS. Per the latest earnings call, ARR tied to these offerings grew three times faster than total ARR in Q3. The Guardian cloud solution remains the key growth driver, delivering >100% ARR growth for the fifth straight quarter, and its customer count more than doubled. My belief is that ARR from cloud and SaaS offerings will continue to be major growth drivers going into 2026 because of the product roadmap ahead. The early-2026 launch of Guardian Investigate will bring investigators (not just examiners) into the same workspace. I think this is a meaningful upgrade because the legacy workflow is still highly fragmented. For perspective, using legacy methods, after examiners extract data in the lab, they deliver it to investigators on USB sticks (a process management said is still used by 60% of investigators). It’s slow and risky, and it’s also the reason why there is a long stretch of backlogs to clear in many agencies.

With Guardian Investigate, both parties are brought into a shared cloud environment. Evidence uploaded by examiners can be accessed instantly by investigators, which cuts down “time-to-evidence” and reduces the manual back-and-forth that slows down the entire process. From an investment angle, I believe this widens the demand pool meaningfully. Think about it. The number of investigators, detectives, and analysts is far larger than the pool of forensic examiners (management believes there is an opportunity to reach hundreds of thousands of these users). If this works, and once these teams adopt the platform, stickiness should jump meaningfully as the workflow becomes dependent on the shared environment (i.e., Guardian Investigate). This also effectively creates a network effect within each agency (more people adopting it leads to more adoption), and that increases the switching cost.

Valuation

In my previous write-up, I noted that margins should move upwards as revenue scales. Indeed, I was right on that front based on the most recent numbers. For context, in Q3, adj. EBITDA grew 20% y/y, with margins expanding 60 bps to ~30%. This gave management the confidence to raise their FY25 adj. EBITDA guidance to a range of $124 million to $127 million. Management also reiterated their expectation for a free cash flow margin of ~30% for the full year.

This guidance upgrade is huge because it proved that my margin assumptions were too conservative. My previous model assumed a 26% adj. EBITDA margin flat through FY27. With what we have today, I think there is a real shot for margins to go higher, likely ~30% in FY27 as revenue continues to scale. Keeping the revenue growth assumption flat at 20%, with the improved margin outlook, I have the business generating ~$203 million in adj. EBITDA in FY27. Assuming the stock valuation remains at the average (25x, down 1x since I last updated), I believe the shares are worth ~$23.

Investment Risk

On the technical front, Sun Corporation still owns a significant stake in CLBT. We don’t know when they will offload their stake (be it to capture profits or for some other reason), but I think this could be a persistent overhang on the stock. The recent Corellium acquisition also faced heavy regulatory scrutiny from CFIUS, which could lead to the deal not going through. While this doesn’t impact the fundamentals, it does create distraction for management. Finally, despite the Q3 rebound, Q4 federal demand is still volatile, and readers should be careful not to extrapolate growth linearly.

Conclusion

I am still positive on the CLBT equity story given the cleaner federal visibility, faster platform adoption, and better margin outlook. There are risks around Sun Corporation’s stake and regulatory noise from the Corellium deal, but none of them really impact the underlying fundamentals. I maintain my "Buy" rating.

data.title: Warner Bros. Discovery: Netflix Improves The Model In One Step
data.description:
Warner Bros. Discovery, Inc. (WBD) and Netflix, Inc. (NFLX) announced that Netflix will buy Warner Bros. Discovery (basically the parts it wants, as in streaming and theater) while shareholders will still receive the spinoff. The official deal from Netflix values the acquired parts at $27.75 and the spinoff is probably worth another (very roughly) $4 or so. I had long thought that the board would not settle for less than $30 per share of value for Warner Bros. Discovery. They may well have exceeded their goal with the roughly $87 billion deal.

I further had long criticized the Netflix model as having issues. This began (with an article) back in 2016 when it became obvious that the "virtuous circle" that Netflix went public based upon was not working. Ever since then I noted that GAAP cash flow and free cash flow have never met those initial expectations. In fact, there was no free cash flow until the last few years. Even then, like now, that free cash flow has been spent in an effort to catch up to the industry leaders that have free cash flow. It makes little to no sense to have market share if you are not generating the cash to go with it. Netflix was in the position of selling a used car for about 10% of its value when it came to cash generation.

Now, what is happening is that Warner Bros. Discovery is receiving the credit for all the work done to improve the situation (vastly) over what was inherited from AT&T (T). As to whether this is the profit that management was going for is an open question. But it vastly improves the Netflix model going forward with both franchises and additional ways to make money off content.

Investors need to remember that valuing stocks (at least in the academic world) was basically a series of models that off came off the idea of discounted cash flow. Now Netflix, with this acquisition, is going to have that discounted cash flow that never happened with the virtuous circle from years ago. As a result, this is going to be a very different Netflix going forward.

Going Forward

This is definitely a game changer for Netflix. But the whole thing needs to be handled correctly for it to work.

Netflix is very good at what it does in the form of producing hits. That needs to continue. But the acquisition, Warner Bros. Discovery (streaming and theater) is also very good at what it does with a fair amount of talent gathered over the last few years combined with brand new infrastructure that was built with the future in mind.

It may be best to figure out how to keep the key people here as a "brain drain" would seriously derail my idea that this is good for Netflix. My own thoughts would be a Warren Buffett-type decentralized model where the talented people keep doing what they do best with a minimal amount of disturbance as long as they meet goals they agree with.

But any investor in Netflix will have to monitor this situation carefully because the benefits can be set back in a very serious manner from a "brain drain."

Given that management will find a way to be successful, it may well be a good idea to consider investing at least some of the cash proceeds in Netflix stock after the acquisition closes. There is usually a time period where the market needs to digest any acquisition, and the stock price sags as a result. Preliminary indications are that is likely to be the case this time.

Mr. Market Had This All Wrong

The other point to make is that contrarian investing still works. But you have got to do your homework and do it thoroughly.

This is a stock that was valued at roughly $10 per share and at times even less in the last fiscal year (as shown above). That happened because the market focused on a lot of business statistics while management went about fixing the business. As shown above, the market gave no credit to management for fixing the business as I noted in the last article and many articles before that.

I do not know how many times I noted that with a leveraged buyout, cash is a major focus because management wants to delever as fast as possible. This was complicated by the fact that things were worse than originally thought once management got their hands on the assets.

There was little to no choice here other than to establish the badly needed infrastructure that enabled proper decision making. But that meant that you had to hope that the business did not get seriously damaged in the meantime. Today's acquisition offer likely means that no serious damage occurred. Instead, it would appear that management added serious value to the business (likely with a lot more value to come).

But the market almost always values based upon history. One of the things I noted early on was a big jump in free cash flow that marks most successful leveraged buyouts. The CFO noted time and again that cash was not a priority for AT&T. Anyone even remotely considering this had to figure that major infrastructure was missing when the CFO noted that collecting and billing past accounts receivable had to happen (and it likely netted a fair amount of money). But that comment alone would likely have led to an investigation that would invalidate a lot of history due to the lack of decision-making infrastructure.

This is in contrast to a market that was valuing the stock based upon a history of write-offs and negative cash flow (or no free cash flow at least). That led to a big undervaluation of future prospects if management fixed the situation correctly. However, the market was going to wait for earnings, cash flow, and free cash flow, to validate the repairs made because Mr. Market has heard this story a whole lot of times before. Only a few of those times were right.

Summary

Most of us had to average down to make a decent profit on Warner Bros. Discovery after the spinoff. Nonetheless, anyone who purchased AT&T stock before the spinoff is doing ok now as the parts have appreciated.

Nonetheless, this is why I make baskets, talk about dollar-cost averaging, and note that the stock could be purchased below the cost of John Malone who was a board member that supported this whole project.

As far as I am concerned, this is not over with by a long shot because the investment can go forward in the form of Netflix stock. That choice will be up to each individual investor. But I will cover Netflix in the future just to see how this whole situation turns out.

I still believe that Warner Bros. Discovery has some appreciation potential to go due to the spinoff and therefore remains a strong buy at today's current price. I look at the deal as a very good one for Netflix shareholders because the part of the company they are acquiring is about to show vastly improved results.

Netflix is acquiring a company that can monetize the Netflix assets in several different ways. So, the benefits to Netflix can extend far beyond the stated benefits.

In the meantime, Warner Bros. Discovery shareholders have a profit over the spinoff price. It is definitely not what I had hoped for. But as noted above I averaged down when I purchased stock as cheaply as $10 per share. Netflix is going to be the beneficiary of the big gains I hoped for. But this happens as often as not, and it is why I make baskets.

I now view Netflix as a speculative buy on the idea that Netflix will manage this properly to achieve a lot of benefits from this acquisition.

Risks

For Netflix holders, this acquisition can be managed badly to fail to achieve the hoped for results.

For Warner Bros. Discovery, I do not view the potential threat of the deal coming undone for any reason because I suspect the future is a whole lot brighter than the past. Nonetheless I also think that the combined company will be a formidable competitor in the marketplace when run correctly. But the key is run correctly. This can become a very strong sell idea if mismanagement surfaces.

This is part of the entertainment industry. Netflix is acquiring franchises that need to be properly monetized and managed. There is no moat where mismanagement is protected for a time. The entertainment industry moves very fast and proper anticipation of the future is essential in this business to properly monetize the assets.
